map.condition: Longest Common Subsequence of two strings of length m, n
Subset-sum / 0-1 Knapsack feasibility with n items, capacity W
Optimal parenthesisation cost of n matrices
All-pairs shortest paths in weighted graph
Number of distinct ways to make change for amount A with coin denominations

formulas.name: Optimal Substructure Property
formulas.whenToUse: Verify DP applicability: optimal solution of the problem is built from optimal solutions of smaller subproblems.

terms.means: optimal value of a (sub)problem instance
combining function (min, max, sum, etc.)

formulas.trap: Greedy also needs optimal substructure; DP additionally requires overlapping subproblems. Confusing the two criteria is a common GATE MCQ trap.

formulas.name: Overlapping Subproblems
formulas.latex: T_{\text{memo}}(n) = O(\text{distinct subproblems}) \times O(\text{work per subproblem})
formulas.plain: T_memo(n) = O(distinct subproblems) x O(work per subproblem)
formulas.whenToUse: Estimate DP time complexity: multiply the number of unique states by the cost of computing each state (excluding recursive calls).

terms.means: time with memoisation / tabulation

formulas.trap: For subset-sum the subproblem count is O(nW), which is pseudo-polynomial (exponential in input bit-length); do not call it polynomial.

formulas.name: DP Table Dimensions (general rule)
formulas.plain: State space = product of ranges of all parameters
formulas.whenToUse: Determine table size and memory complexity before implementing.

terms.means: total number of distinct subproblems = table entries

formulas.trap: For 2-D DP (e.g. LCS with m x n table, subset-sum with n x W table), students often confuse rows and columns leading to wrong sizes.

formulas.name: Subset-Sum DP Recurrence
formulas.whenToUse: Decide if a subset of the first i elements sums to exactly w.

terms.means: value of i-th element
target sum
true if some subset of first i elements sums to w

formulas.trap: The table is boolean (true/false), not an integer count. Also, S[i][0] = true for all i (empty subset sums to 0).

formulas.name: Subset-Sum Time & Space Complexity
formulas.latex: \text{Time} = O(nW), \quad \text{Space} = O(nW) \text{ (or } O(W) \text{ with rolling array)}
formulas.plain: Time = O(n*W),  Space = O(n*W) or O(W)
formulas.whenToUse: Analyse the efficiency of a subset-sum / 0-1 knapsack DP.

terms.means: number of elements in set S
target sum (or knapsack capacity)

formulas.trap: O(nW) is pseudo-polynomial, not polynomial, because W can be exponential in the number of bits used to encode it.

formulas.name: 0-1 Knapsack DP Recurrence
formulas.plain: K[i][w] = max(K[i-1][w], v_i + K[i-1][w - w_i])  when w_i <= w
formulas.whenToUse: Maximise total value of items packed into a knapsack of capacity W.

terms.means: value of item i
weight of item i
current capacity being considered

formulas.trap: Each item can be included at most once (0-1). For unbounded knapsack use K[i][w-w_i] (not K[i-1][w-w_i]).

formulas.name: LCS Recurrence
formulas.latex: c[i][j] = \begin{cases} 0 & i=0 \text{ or } j=0 \\ c[i-1][j-1]+1 & x_i = y_j \\ \max(c[i-1][j],\, c[i][j-1]) & x_i \neq y_j \end{cases}
formulas.plain: c[i][j] = 0 if i=0 or j=0; c[i-1][j-1]+1 if x_i==y_j; max(c[i-1][j], c[i][j-1]) otherwise
formulas.whenToUse: Find the length of the longest common subsequence of sequences X[1..m] and Y[1..n].

terms.means: LCS length of X[1..i] and Y[1..j]
i-th and j-th characters of X and Y

formulas.trap: LCS is a subsequence (not subarray). Elements need not be contiguous. Longest Common Substring requires a different recurrence (reset to 0 on mismatch).

formulas.name: LCS Time & Space Complexity
formulas.latex: \text{Time} = O(mn), \quad \text{Space} = O(mn) \text{ (or } O(\min(m,n)) \text{ with two-row trick)}
formulas.plain: Time = O(m*n),  Space = O(m*n) or O(min(m,n))
formulas.whenToUse: Assess the cost of LCS computation on sequences of length m and n.

terms.means: lengths of the two input sequences

formulas.trap: The 2-D table requires O(mn) space; if asked for the actual LCS string (not just length), you need the full table (or use Hirschberg's algorithm).

formulas.name: MCM DP Recurrence
formulas.latex: m[i][j] = \min_{i \le k < j}\left\{m[i][k] + m[k+1][j] + p_{i-1}\,p_k\,p_j\right\}, \quad m[i][i]=0
formulas.plain: m[i][j] = min over k in [i,j-1] of { m[i][k] + m[k+1][j] + p_(i-1)*p_k*p_j },  m[i][i] = 0
formulas.whenToUse: Find the minimum scalar multiplications to compute the product of matrices A_i ... A_j where A_i has dimensions p_(i-1) x p_i.

terms.means: minimum cost to multiply matrices A_i through A_j
dimensions: A_i has p_(i-1) rows, split matrix has p_k cols, A_j has p_j cols
split point: (A_i...A_k)(A_(k+1)...A_j)

formulas.trap: The split cost p_(i-1)*p_k*p_j uses THREE dimension values. Forgetting the outer dimension p_(i-1) (or p_j) is the classic GATE error.

formulas.name: MCM Time & Space Complexity
formulas.plain: Time = O(n^3),  Space = O(n^2),  n = number of matrices
formulas.whenToUse: State the complexity of the DP algorithm for n matrices.

terms.means: number of matrices in the chain

formulas.trap: There are n-1 multiplication operations between n matrices. Confusing n (matrices) with n-1 (products) causes off-by-one in dimension arrays.

formulas.name: Number of Parenthesisations (Catalan Number)
formulas.whenToUse: Explain why brute-force enumeration of parenthesisations is exponential, motivating DP.

terms.means: (n-1)-th Catalan number
number of matrices

formulas.trap: P(n) grows exponentially; naive brute-force is infeasible for large n, but DP reduces it to O(n^3).

formulas.name: Cost of Multiplying Two Sub-chains
formulas.latex: (p \times q) \cdot (q \times r) \text{ costs } p \cdot q \cdot r \text{ scalar multiplications}
formulas.plain: Multiplying a (p x q) matrix with a (q x r) matrix costs p*q*r scalar multiplications
formulas.whenToUse: Evaluate the cost of one specific parenthesisation split — used when manually computing m[i][k] + m[k+1][j] + p_(i-1)*p_k*p_j.

terms.means: row, shared, and column dimensions

formulas.trap: The result matrix is p x r (not p x q or q x r). Dimensions must be compatible: inner dimensions must match.

formulas.name: Floyd-Warshall Recurrence
formulas.whenToUse: Compute shortest paths between all pairs of vertices using intermediate vertex k.

terms.means: shortest path from i to j using only vertices {1,...,k} as intermediates
index of the intermediate vertex being considered

formulas.trap: Floyd-Warshall works with negative edge weights but NOT negative cycles. Detect negative cycles by checking if d[i][i] < 0 after running.

formulas.name: Floyd-Warshall Complexity
formulas.whenToUse: State complexity of all-pairs shortest path via DP on a graph with V vertices.

terms.means: number of vertices in the graph

formulas.trap: Running Dijkstra from each vertex gives O(V(E + V log V)), which is better for sparse graphs. Floyd-Warshall is better or simpler for dense graphs.

formulas.name: Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS)
formulas.latex: L[i] = 1 + \max_{j < i,\; a_j < a_i} L[j], \quad \text{LIS} = \max_i L[i]
formulas.plain: L[i] = 1 + max{ L[j] : j < i and a[j] < a[i] },  LIS = max(L[i])
formulas.whenToUse: Find the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence of array a[1..n].

terms.means: length of LIS ending at index i
i-th element of the array

formulas.trap: Naive DP is O(n^2); the patience-sort / binary-search approach is O(n log n). GATE questions sometimes ask which is faster.

formulas.name: Coin Change (Minimum Coins)
formulas.plain: dp[a] = 1 + min{ dp[a - c] : c in C, c <= a },  dp[0] = 0
formulas.whenToUse: Find minimum number of coins from set C to make amount a.

terms.means: target amount
set of coin denominations
minimum coins needed for amount a

formulas.trap: Greedy (always pick largest coin) does NOT always give the optimal answer for arbitrary denominations — DP is required.

formulas.name: Edit Distance (Levenshtein) Recurrence
formulas.latex: e[i][j] = \begin{cases} i & j=0 \\ j & i=0 \\ e[i-1][j-1] & x_i=y_j \\ 1+\min(e[i-1][j],\, e[i][j-1],\, e[i-1][j-1]) & x_i \neq y_j \end{cases}
formulas.plain: e[i][j] = i if j=0; j if i=0; e[i-1][j-1] if x_i==y_j; 1 + min(e[i-1][j], e[i][j-1], e[i-1][j-1]) otherwise
formulas.whenToUse: Find minimum insert/delete/replace operations to convert string X[1..m] to Y[1..n].

terms.means: edit distance between X[1..i] and Y[1..j]

formulas.trap: Three operations (insert, delete, replace) correspond to three recurrence terms. Omitting any one gives wrong answers.

formulas.name: DP vs Greedy: When to Use Which
formulas.latex: \text{Greedy: locally optimal} \Rightarrow \text{globally optimal (exchange argument)} \quad \text{DP: overlapping subproblems + optimal substructure}
formulas.plain: Greedy: local choice => global optimum (exchange argument).  DP: overlapping subproblems + optimal substructure needed.
formulas.whenToUse: Decide whether a problem should be solved with greedy or DP.

terms.means: proof technique showing no swap of choices can improve greedy solution

formulas.trap: Coin change, 0-1 knapsack, and subset-sum all require DP. Activity selection and fractional knapsack are greedy. Mixing them is a classic GATE trap.
